label: Client (Web/Mobile)
description: Web browsers and mobile apps through which customers browse, search, and purchase products.
techStack: React (Web), Native Apps (iOS/Android), A/B Testing Framework
role: Renders the storefront, product pages, and checkout flow. Each page is optimized for conversion through continuous experimentation.
whyChosen: React enables rapid iteration on the web storefront. Native mobile apps provide smoother shopping experiences with push notification and offline catalog support.

cons: Feature parity across platforms is challenging

alternatives.name: Next.js SSR
alternatives.context: Server-rendered React for faster initial loads

label: Elastic Load Balancer
description: AWS-native load balancer distributing incoming traffic across multiple availability zones.
role: Distributes millions of requests per second across healthy service instances, performs SSL termination, and integrates with AWS auto-scaling for elastic capacity.
whyChosen: As the creators of AWS, Amazon leverages their own infrastructure. ALB provides L7 routing, sticky sessions, and seamless integration with ECS/EKS.

cons: Less customizable than software load balancers

label: API Gateway Services
description: Microservice gateway handling authentication, rate limiting, and request routing to hundreds of backend services.
techStack: Custom Java services, AWS API Gateway
role: Authenticates requests, applies rate limits, transforms payloads, and routes to the correct domain services. Handles versioning and backward compatibility.
whyChosen: Amazon's scale requires custom gateway logic for complex routing rules (marketplace vs retail, regional regulations, seller APIs).

pros: Custom routing for complex multi-tenant scenarios
cons: Single point of failure risk without proper redundancy

alternatives.name: Kong
alternatives.context: Open-source API gateway with plugin ecosystem

label: Product Catalog Service
description: Manages the catalog of hundreds of millions of products with real-time pricing, availability, and attribute data.
role: Central source of truth for product information. Serves product details, pricing, and inventory status to all consumer-facing and internal services.
whyChosen: A dedicated product service allows independent scaling and data model evolution for the massive catalog without impacting other services.

pros: Consistent product data across all channels

label: Order Processing Service
description: Manages the order lifecycle from cart to delivery, orchestrating payment, inventory, and fulfillment workflows.
role: Coordinates the complex multi-step purchase flow: inventory reservation, payment processing, fraud detection, and fulfillment routing.
whyChosen: Order processing requires strong consistency guarantees and saga-pattern orchestration across multiple bounded contexts (payment, inventory, shipping).

pros: Reliable saga orchestration for distributed transactions
cons: High operational criticality — downtime = lost revenue

label: Search Engine
description: Powers product search across hundreds of millions of listings with sub-second response times.
role: Processes search queries, applies ML-based ranking, handles typo correction, synonyms, and personalized result ordering based on purchase history.
whyChosen: Amazon's A9 search is a competitive differentiator. Custom search allows integration of purchase intent signals and advertiser-bid ranking that generic engines can't provide.

pros: Real-time index updates for price/availability changes
cons: Balancing organic vs paid results transparency

label: DynamoDB
description: Fully managed NoSQL database providing single-digit millisecond performance at any scale.
techStack: Amazon DynamoDB, Global Tables, DAX Cache
role: Primary database for high-throughput workloads: shopping carts, session state, user preferences. Global Tables provide multi-region active-active replication.
whyChosen: Born from Amazon's Dynamo paper. Provides predictable performance with automatic scaling — critical for Black Friday-scale traffic spikes.

cons: Limited query patterns (must design for access patterns)
Cost can spike with high throughput

label: SQS / SNS / EventBridge
description: Managed messaging services for asynchronous event-driven communication between microservices.
role: Decouples services through message queues (SQS) and pub/sub topics (SNS). EventBridge provides event routing with content-based filtering for complex workflows.
whyChosen: Managed messaging eliminates the operational overhead of running Kafka/RabbitMQ clusters while providing at-least-once delivery guarantees.

pros: SQS provides dead-letter queues for error handling
cons: Higher per-message cost than self-hosted Kafka
Less control over partitioning and ordering

label: ElastiCache (Redis)
description: In-memory caching layer providing microsecond read latency for frequently accessed data.
techStack: Amazon ElastiCache for Redis, cluster mode
role: Caches product details, session state, and search results. Reduces database load and speeds up page rendering for high-traffic product pages.
whyChosen: Redis provides rich data structures (sorted sets for rankings, hashes for session data) beyond simple key-value caching, with managed cluster mode for scaling.

cons: Data loss risk on node failure without persistence

label: Recommendation Engine
description: ML-powered personalization engine generating 'Customers who bought...' and personalized homepage recommendations.
techStack: Custom ML, SageMaker, Collaborative Filtering, Deep Learning
role: Analyzes purchase history, browsing behavior, and contextual signals to generate real-time product recommendations that drive 35%+ of revenue.
whyChosen: Personalized recommendations are a massive revenue driver. Custom models allow optimization for conversion and basket size rather than generic engagement metrics.

pros: Real-time personalization based on session behavior
cons: Massive compute for training at catalog scale

alternatives.name: Amazon Personalize
alternatives.context: Managed version of their own tech
